description: Bold Nigerian tomato stew made with fresh or frozen Titus mackerel. Faster than meat stew but demands careful handling — fish breaks easily and the base must be properly fried before the fish ever touches it.

ingredients.name: Titus mackerel (fresh or frozen)
ingredients.note: Fresh is best. Frozen works — defrost fully and pat dry before seasoning. Cut into steaks about 4cm thick.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Fish stew does not need excessive heat — 1 is enough for most.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for seasoning the fish, 1 into the blend, 1 sliced for frying the base.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Less oil than meat stew — fish releases its own fat into the base.

ingredients.name: tomato paste
ingredients.note: For depth of colour and flavour.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: For both seasoning the fish and flavouring the stew.

ingredients.name: bay leaves
ingredients.note: Add to the stew base — subtle herby depth.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: 1 for the fish, 1 for the stew base.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Particularly complementary to fish stew — deepens the seafood dimension.

ingredients.name: garlic
ingredients.note: For seasoning the fish. Do not add to the stew base — it overpowers the fish.

ingredients.name: lemon or lime juice
ingredients.note: For washing and marinating the fish. Removes the fishy smell significantly.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season the fish well. Season the stew base separately and carefully — fish adds its own salt.

steps.title: Clean and Season the Fish
steps.instruction: If using whole fish, gut and clean thoroughly. Cut into steaks about 4cm thick — too thin and they fall apart in the stew. Wash the fish steaks in water with lemon or lime juice to remove the fishy smell. Rinse with clean water and pat completely dry with paper towels. Season with curry powder, white pepper, garlic if using, 1 crushed seasoning cube, and salt. Rest for 15 minutes.
steps.sensory_cue: Seasoned fish should smell clean and aromatic, not overwhelmingly fishy. The flesh should look firm and hold its shape when handled.
steps.failure_risk: Wet fish does not fry properly — it steams and falls apart. Pat it as dry as possible. Seasoning wet fish also means the seasoning runs off instead of adhering.

steps.title: Shallow-Fry the Fish
steps.instruction: Heat half the oil in a wide pan over high heat until very hot. Carefully lower fish steaks into the oil — they should sizzle immediately and loudly. Fry for 3–4 minutes per side until golden-brown and a crust has formed. Do not move them during frying — let the crust develop. Remove carefully with a wide spatula or fish slice. Set aside.
steps.sensory_cue: The crust should be golden-brown and slightly firm — this is the structural barrier that keeps the fish intact in the stew. It should smell beautifully savoury, not raw.
steps.failure_risk: Moving the fish too soon breaks it. Let the crust form before flipping. If it sticks, it is not ready — wait another 30 seconds. Do not cover the pan — steam breaks the crust.

steps.title: Build the Stew Base
steps.instruction: In the same pan with the fish-flavoured oil (add the remaining oil), fry sliced onions over medium-high until soft and golden. Add tomato paste and fry 3 minutes until it darkens. Pour in the blended tomatoes, tatashe, and scotch bonnet. Increase to high heat and fry the base, stirring every 90 seconds, until thick, concentrated, and the oil has fully risen to the surface.
steps.sensory_cue: Thick, paste-like base. Oil clearly on top. Dark orange-red colour. Raw tomato smell gone. The fish oil left in the pan will add a subtle seafood note to the base — this is good.
steps.failure_risk: Fish stew base needs to be fully fried before adding the fish — once the fish goes in you cannot stir aggressively without breaking it. The base is your only window to get it right.

steps.title: Season the Base
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Add curry powder, thyme, ground crayfish, bay leaves, and 1 crushed seasoning cube. Add a small amount of salt — go carefully here, the fish will release more salt as it finishes cooking. Stir everything in well. Add a small splash of water (about ¼ cup) to loosen the base slightly — fish stew should be saucier than meat stew.
steps.sensory_cue: The base should smell full and complex. Adding the crayfish will noticeably deepen the aroma. Looser than meat stew — the fish needs sauce to cook in without burning.
steps.failure_risk: Do not over-salt here. Titus mackerel is naturally salty and will release more as it cooks. You can always add salt — you cannot take it away.

steps.title: Add Fish and Finish Gently
steps.instruction: Carefully lay the fried fish steaks into the stew base. Do not stir — gently spoon the sauce over the fish pieces to coat them. Cover with a lid and cook on low heat for 10–12 minutes. The fish will release its juices into the stew, enriching it further. Gently turn each piece once — use a spatula, not a spoon — halfway through. Remove bay leaves. Taste and adjust salt at the very end.
steps.sensory_cue: The stew around the fish should look rich, glossy, and deeply coloured. The fish should be fully cooked through — press gently and it should flake slightly. The sauce should smell like the sea met the market.
steps.failure_risk: Stirring breaks the fish into pieces — you lose the visual and textural integrity of the whole steaks. Spoon the sauce over instead of mixing. Do not overcook — fish goes from perfect to dry very quickly.
